subject: Firebase creds for discoveredOrganizers upload — keychain slots ready
body:
Booker — Firebase service account creds are now in macOS keychain on Toby's Mac mini. Both slots are verified. Dual-slot pattern is in place (Quinn/Toby caught an earlier mislabel — see notes).

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 KEYCHAIN SLOTS (both verified with hardcoded project_id assertion)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

TEST:
  service name: firebase-tangotiempo-test-sa
  project_id:   tangotiempo-257ff
  client_email: firebase-adminsdk-gx3rl@tangotiempo-257ff.iam.gserviceaccount.com

PROD:
  service name: firebase-tangotiempoprod-sa
  project_id:   tangotiempoprod
  client_email: firebase-adminsdk-qtaqd@tangotiempoprod.iam.gserviceaccount.com

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 CRITICAL: security -w returns HEX, not raw bytes
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

The JSON contains newlines inside the PEM private_key, so macOS `security find-generic-password -w` outputs the value as a hex string with no 0x prefix. You MUST decode it before parsing as JSON.

Shell:
  security find-generic-password -a "$USER" -s "firebase-tangotiempo-test-sa" -w | xxd -r -p

Node/TypeScript helper (bake this into your loader):

  import { execSync } from 'child_process';

  interface ServiceAccount {
    project_id: string;
    client_email: string;
    private_key: string;
    [key: string]: unknown;
  }

  function loadFirebaseCred(env: 'test' | 'prod'): ServiceAccount {
    const slot = env === 'prod'
      ? 'firebase-tangotiempoprod-sa'
      : 'firebase-tangotiempo-test-sa';
    const hex = execSync(
      `security find-generic-password -a "$USER" -s "${slot}" -w`,
      { encoding: 'utf-8' }
    ).trim();
    const json = Buffer.from(hex, 'hex').toString('utf-8');
    const cred = JSON.parse(json) as ServiceAccount;

    // HARD GUARDRAIL — refuse to run if slot has wrong project
    const expected = env === 'prod' ? 'tangotiempoprod' : 'tangotiempo-257ff';
    if (cred.project_id !== expected) {
      throw new Error(
        `SLOT MISMATCH: --env=${env} expected project ${expected}, ` +
        `keychain slot ${slot} contains ${cred.project_id}. Refusing to continue.`
      );
    }
    return cred;
  }

  // Usage in your upload script:
  const cred = loadFirebaseCred(args.env);
  admin.initializeApp({ credential: admin.credential.cert(cred as any) });

This guardrail is NON-NEGOTIABLE. We already had one near-miss where the PROD cred was stored in the TEST slot — the dual-slot system is the last line of defense against cross-env writes.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 FULTON'S FIRESTORE SCHEMA (as sent Apr 6)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Collection: discoveredOrganizers

Document shape:
{
  name: string,                      // 'Beth Nicholson'
  fbProfileUrl: string,              // 'https://www.facebook.com/beth.nicholson.902'
  email: string | null,              // null for most
  website: string | null,
  city: string | null,               // 'Vestavia Hills'
  state: string | null,              // 'AL'
  country: string,                   // default 'US'
  hostType: string,                  // INDIVIDUAL | ORGANIZATION | EVENT_SERIES
  eventCount: number,                // canonical merged count
  sampleEventTitles: string[],       // up to ~5 representative titles
  outreachStatus: 'not_contacted',   // initial value
  contactedAt: null,
  contactedVia: null,
  responseAt: null,
  orgToken: null,                    // populated later by Fulton's generate-link
  appliedAt: null,
  mongoOrgId: null,                  // populated after MongoDB signup
  discoveredAt: Timestamp,           // now
  lastSeenAt: Timestamp,             // last time seen in a scrape
  source: 'fb-conditioner'           // will rebrand to 'booker' after rename
}

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 IDEMPOTENT DOC IDs
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Re-runs must not create duplicates. Use a deterministic hash of (name + city):

  import { createHash } from 'crypto';
  function docId(name: string, city: string | null): string {
    const key = `${name.toLowerCase().trim()}|${(city || '').toLowerCase().trim()}`;
    return createHash('sha256').update(key).digest('hex').substring(0, 20);
  }

Then: firestore.collection('discoveredOrganizers').doc(docId(name, city)).set({...}, { merge: true })

Merge semantics: if the doc exists, update scalar fields but DO NOT overwrite outreachStatus/contactedAt/orgToken/mongoOrgId — those are owned by Fulton's outreach pipeline.

Safer: use { merge: true } for discovery fields, and skip the outreach-owned fields entirely. Or compute a diff.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 POC SCOPE — TEST FIRST, ONE RECORD
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

First run:
  node scripts/upload-organizers.ts --env=test --limit=1 --filter-name='Toby Balsley'

That should write exactly ONE document to tangotiempo-257ff Firestore, project discoveredOrganizers collection.

After that, I (Fulton) will E2E test on calendarbeaf-test.azurewebsites.net:
  1. POST /api/outreach/generate-link with discoveredOrganizerId pointing at the uploaded doc
  2. GET /api/outreach/resolve-token?token=X — verify prefill enrichment picked up the Firestore fields
  3. POST /api/outreach/track event=link_opened — verify write path

If that works end-to-end on TEST, then we'll do a limited PROD upload.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 SOURCE DATA
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Read from Booker SQLite view: outreach_organizers (already clean, deduped)
Write back: firestore_doc_id column in that view (or a companion table) so re-runs know what's already uploaded

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
 OPEN QUESTIONS
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

1. Is Firestore enabled on tangotiempo-257ff in Native mode? (If not, your first write will fail — create the database in Firebase Console first)
2. Does the TEST service account have Cloud Datastore User role? (Admin SDK usually bypasses this, but worth checking)
3. Do you have firestore-admin npm package installed already, or need to add it?

Report back when the POC upload succeeds and I'll start the E2E on TEST.
